summary: Gruber dissects the Google-Motorola acquisition and pushes back hard against Dan Lyons's triumphalist framing that Google executed a brilliant rope-a-dope strategy. He argues that Google's $12.5 billion purchase of an unprofitable, second-rate phone maker wasn't a masterstroke but a sign of desperation — Motorola had Google by the balls and extracted a massive premium by threatening patent warfare against other Android makers. Gruber marshals reporting from Om Malik and others to show the deal was thrown together in five weeks after Google lost the Nortel patent auction, not a long-planned chess move. He notes Google spent nearly two years of profits on a company with declining sales and no recent profitability, while taking on a $2.5 billion reverse breakup fee. The piece concludes that Apple and Microsoft likely feel good about forcing Google into this expensive, awkward position.
key_points: Motorola leveraged its patent portfolio and threats against fellow Android makers to force Google into a full acquisition at a steep premium
Google's $12.5 billion spend represents nearly two years of total company profits for an unprofitable, declining handset maker
Reporting suggests the deal was assembled in five weeks after Google lost the Nortel auction, contradicting the 'brilliant long-term strategy' narrative
The acquisition creates tension with Samsung and HTC, Google's two most important Android partners
The unusual $2.5 billion reverse breakup fee signals either extreme confidence or desperation
money_quotes: After bidding over $4 billion for something — anything — you just look childish when, only after losing the auction, you then claim you didn't really want the thing anyway.
I think Motorola knew they had Google by the balls.
Confidence is one possibility. Desperation is another.

If every Motorola product was removed from every store in the world tonight, and replaced by the closest equivalent devices from HTC and Samsung, would the mobile landscape be significantly different tomorrow?
Google just spent almost two years of its profits to buy a second-rate phone maker that itself is unprofitable, almost went bankrupt, and is arguably only the third-best maker of Android devices.
That position was a weak one, and to pretend otherwise is to deny the obvious.
Motorola held out for a full acquisition at a premium far above the company's actual value, and threatened to go after its sibling Android partners if Google didn't acquiesce.
key_insight: Google's acquisition of Motorola wasn't a strategic masterstroke but an expensive defensive move forced by Motorola's leverage over Android's patent vulnerability.
